questions.prompt: Highest-leverage brand decision in an iOS app respecting conventions?

choices.text: Accent color (`tintColor`). iOS automatically applies your accent to: button text/icons, links, switch on-state, selected tab bar items, slider tracks, refresh control, segmented control, navigation back button — every interactive UI element in one consistent color. Suddenly the whole app has your brand thumbprint while everything else remains conventionally native. Single line of code (`.tint(.brandColor)` in SwiftUI), applies system-wide. Compared to custom fonts (cost: licensing, performance, dynamic type complexity, accessibility risk) or custom controls (cost: rebuilding accessibility, dark mode, haptics, RTL): accent color is free, instant, and risk-free. Pick wisely: sufficient contrast in both modes; distinctive (avoid generic system blue); tested for color-blindness. One color choice + SF Pro + SF Symbols + your app icon = native iOS app with clear brand identity. Apps that try to brand harder (custom everything) usually feel worse, not more on-brand.

questions.explanation: Accent color via tintColor — applies system-wide automatically. Free, distinctive, conventional. Highest leverage.

questions.prompt: Designer wants to replace UISwitch with a custom-styled toggle for brand consistency. Trade-offs?

choices.text: Probably not worth it. Replacing UISwitch loses: VoiceOver support (custom toggle needs accessibility identifiers + traits manually); haptic feedback (UISwitch auto-haptics on toggle); dark mode adaptation (system colors adjust automatically); Dynamic Type sizing (system controls scale); RTL layout (automatic for system); Reduce Motion respect (built in); built-in animations (smooth on iOS, hard to match); future iOS visual updates (UISwitch evolves with iOS releases; custom is stuck). Cost: re-implement all of the above (weeks of careful work + ongoing maintenance) for the brand-consistency upside of a slightly different toggle look. Better: tint your UISwitch with brand accent color (`.tint(.brand)` or `onTintColor`); now it has your brand's color while keeping all platform features. The HIG explicitly recommends using system controls; custom controls should solve genuine product needs, not aesthetic preferences. The 90% rule: system control + accent color is the answer.

questions.explanation: Loses VoiceOver, haptics, dark mode, Dynamic Type, RTL, animations. Tint the UISwitch instead.
